perturbation.original_text: The receiving party shall inform its Associates of the confidential nature of the Confidential Information and shall instruct them to treat the information confidentially. The receiving party shall be responsible for any breach of this Agreement by its Associates and agrees, at its sole expense, to take all commercially reasonable measures to restrain its Associates from disclosure or use of the Confidential Information in breach of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The receiving party shall inform its Associates of the confidential nature of the Confidential Information and shall instruct them to treat the information confidentially.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the clause holding the receiving party responsible for breaches by its Associates weakens the confidentiality protection. In Delaware, under agency law, a principal (the receiving party) can be held liable for the actions of its agents (Associates). By removing the clause, the contract attempts to limit this liability.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Delaware Agency Law Principles (Restatement (Third) of Agency)
perturbation.law_explanation: Restatement (Third) of Agency Section 7.03 states that a principal is subject to liability to a third party harmed by an agent's conduct when the agent acts within the scope of their authority or when the principal ratifies the agent's conduct. By deleting the sentence where the receiving party agrees to be responsible for any breach of this agreement by its associates and take commercially reasonable measures to prevent the breach, the agreement contradicts general agency principles under Delaware law, attempting to shield the receiving party from liability for its Associate's actions related to confidential information. This might not be enforceable in court.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: agency | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you agency Agency law is a common law doctrine controlling relationships between agents and principals . A principal-agent relationship is created when the agent is given authority to act on behalf of the principal. An agreement made by an agent is binding on the principal so long as the agreement was within the authority actually granted to the agent or reasonably perceived by a third party . The two forms of actual authority include: Express authority : An agent has express authority to take any actions requested by the principal as well as authority to take any actions inherently necessary to accomplish those requests. Implied authority : An agent has implied authority to take any action the principal’s conduct indicates the agent should do. Furthermore, failure by the principal to object to an agent’s prior actions may create implied authority to repeat those actions in the future. An agent does not have implied authority to do anything expressly prohibited by the principal. In addition to actual authority, a principal may be bound by the actions of an agent if apparent authority existed. Apparent authority : An agent has apparent authority when, despite no existing authority, a third party reasonably infers that someone is authorized to act on the principal’s behalf due to the conduct of the principal. Unlike actual authority, a principal can be bound by an agent’s act made with apparent authority even if they explicitly stated that the agent could not do that act. Furthermore, a person possessing a widely recognized title like Hiring Director has apparent authority to accomplish anything a reasonable person would believe that title entails. An agent acting with apparent authority is known as an ostensible agent . Additionally, principals can be held liable for the torts of their agents under the doctrine of vicarious liability . A principal is always liable for torts committed while the agent completes their official responsibilities. For torts occurring outside of official duties , the liability of the principal depends on whether the agent’s tort occurred during a frolic or a detour. A principal is liable for the “detours” of their agent but not for the “frolics.” The primary considerations many courts use to determine if an act was a frolic or a detour include how much control the principal exerts over the agent’s actions and who economically benefits from the agent’s actions. See: Pyne v. Witmer, 129 Ill. 2d 351 (1989) . perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the receiving party's responsibility for breaches by its Associates, which contradicts agency principles. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states, 'A principal-agent relationship is created when the agent is given authority to act on behalf of the principal. An agreement made by an agent is binding on the principal so long as the agreement was within the authority actually granted to the agent'. By removing the clause holding the receiving party responsible, the agreement attempts to limit liability for the actions of its agents (Associates), directly conflicting with established agency law where a principal can be held responsible for the actions of its agents, especially when acting within their authority.

perturbation.original_text: Notwithstanding the foregoing, the receiving party and its Associates may retain any Confidential Information to the extent required pursuant to the regulatory compliance or record retention policies of such receiving party or Associate.
perturbation.changed_text: Notwithstanding the foregoing, the receiving party may retain any Confidential Information according to internal record retention policies.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'and its Associates' and 'regulatory compliance', the revised text now implies that only the receiving party can retain confidential information. The removal of 'regulatory compliance' removes an important safeguard.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Sarbanes-Oxley Act of 2002 (SOX) mandates specific record retention policies for publicly traded companies. By removing the reference to 'regulatory compliance,' the amended clause creates a loophole where Associates may discard confidential information, potentially violating SOX if those records are relevant to financial reporting and audits. SOX requires companies and their associates to retain documents for a certain period. If an Associate destroyed financial records from the disclosing party, it could potentially be a violation of this federal law.

perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the phrase 'and its Associates' and 'regulatory compliance'. The law explanation argues this creates a loophole for associates to discard confidential information. The original text mandates that both the receiving party and its associates retain confidential information to the extent required by regulatory compliance policies. The omission of 'and its Associates' directly contradicts this requirement, potentially violating SOX if those records are relevant to financial reporting and audits. Scraped Law Snippet 1, 15 USC 7266, discusses the enhanced review of disclosures by issuers, suggesting a regulatory environment where record retention is important. The modified text weakens this by limiting the retention obligation to only the receiving party, thus creating a potential contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Each party agrees that it will take commercially reasonable measures to protect the confidentiality of, and to avoid having Confidential Information of the other party enter the public domain, become publicly available, or be held by persons not authorized hereunder to have such Confidential Information, which measures shall include at least the same degree of care that the receiving party utilizes to protect its own Confidential Information of a similar nature.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party agrees that it will take measures to protect the confidentiality of, and to avoid having Confidential Information of the other party enter the public domain, become publicly available, or be held by persons not authorized hereunder to have such Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the 'commercially reasonable' qualifier weakens the obligation. Also, removing the 'same degree of care' benchmark lowers the required standard of care.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Delaware Trade Secrets Act requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. By removing the 'commercially reasonable' qualifier and the benchmark of 'same degree of care,' the agreement potentially contradicts the DTSA's requirement for reasonable measures. The standard of care may now be seen as lower than what is required for trade secret protection, especially if the receiving party does not treat its own confidential information with great care. A court may find that the modified clause does not fulfill the requirements to protect trade secrets.

Trade Secrets Â§ 2001. Definitions. The following words, terms and phrases, when used in this chapter, shall have the meanings ascribed to them in this section, except where the context clearly indicates a different meaning: (1) âImproper meansâ shall include theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. (2) âMisappropriationâ shall mean: a. Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or b. Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: 1. Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or 2. At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade was: A. Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; B. Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or C. Derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or 3. Before a material change of the personâs position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (3) âPersonâ shall mean a natural person, corporation, statutory trust, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, association, joint venture, government, governmental subdivision or agency, or any other legal or commercial entity. (4) âTrade secretâ shall mean information, including a formula, pattern, compilation, program, device, method, technique or process, that: a. Derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not b
perturbation.justification: The original text requires 'commercially reasonable measures' and a 'same degree of care' standard for protecting confidential information, while the changed text removes these qualifiers, weakening the protection. The Law Explanation argues this contradicts the Delaware Trade Secrets Act (DTSA), which requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'misappropriation' and 'improper means,' highlighting the importance of maintaining secrecy. By removing the 'commercially reasonable' and 'same degree of care' standards, the modified contract may fail to meet the 'reasonable efforts' standard required for trade secret protection under Delaware law, potentially enabling misappropriation through means that might not have been considered sufficient under the original, stricter terms. The omission creates a loophole, making it easier to argue that sufficient measures weren't legally mandated, hence a contradiction exists.
